message:
chore(beads): file 2 HIGH review beads — Categorical coverage, fuzz memory/time bounds

Adds two P1/high-priority review beads surfaced during the latest
cc-pandas conformance+fuzz audit sweep:

- frankenpandas-2ssw (review, conformance, coverage, categorical):
  README capability row advertises Categorical dtype as "Yes / Yes /
  Yes (metadata layer)" but the conformance fixture corpus contains
  exactly ONE categorical operation (series_categorical_from_codes)
  out of ~50+ pandas Categorical methods/properties/accessors. The
  bead enumerates the full first-wave packet suite (FP-P2D-CAT-001..070)
  covering constructors, mutation, comparison, sort/groupby/concat
  interaction, error paths, plus the oracle handler + live-oracle
  test uplift and README/DISCREPANCIES follow-ups. Prerequisites: br-d6xa
  (CI pandas install) and br-urhy (oracle tests).

- frankenpandas-auys (review, fuzz, memory-bounds, dos):
  Every fuzz target has an INPUT size cap (64KB–512KB) — anti-pattern
  #2 is partially addressed — but NO cap on peak allocation, result
  DataFrame size, or wall-clock time per invocation. Amplification
  vectors include zip-compressed Excel sharedStrings XML (512KB in
  decompresses to ~500MB), deeply-nested JSON recursion, parquet
  rowgroup metadata claiming 2B rows preallocating a Vec<Int64>,
  and cross-join SQL result sets. Bead prescribes libFuzzer
  `-rss_limit_mb=16 -timeout=1` per-bin flags plus a tracking-allocator
  helper `cap_memory_and_time(MAX_ALLOC_PER_RUN, MAX_WALL_CLOCK)` with
  known-bomb regression seeds. Related: br-zjme (fuzz CI surfaces),
  br-liai (differential vs pandas alignment), br-k05t (scale fixtures).

Both beads capture the full cross-reference map (related beads, fix
shape, blast radius, labels) so the work can be picked up without
re-running the audit.

Co-Authored-By: Claude <noreply@anthropic.com>

+{"id":"frankenpandas-2ssw","title":"[HIGH] conformance: Categorical dtype has 1 fixture for ~50+ pandas methods — README 'metadata layer' claim under-covered","description":"Applying /testing-conformance-harnesses skill's Coverage Accounting Matrix rule — specifically to pandas's Categorical dtype surface.

**Observation.** README capability row: "Categorical dtype | Yes | Yes | Yes (metadata layer)". README prose claims 50+ string ops and pandas-compatible cat accessor.

Reality check:
- grep for "cat_" / "categorical" in fixture `"operation"` fields across all 1249 fixtures returns exactly ONE operation name: `series_categorical_from_codes`.
- That's ONE fixture covering ONE constructor.

pandas's Categorical public surface (from pd.Categorical.__dict__ introspection):
  Constructors (6): Categorical(values), Categorical(values, categories=, ordered=),
                   Categorical.from_codes(codes, categories, ordered=),
                   pd.CategoricalDtype, pd.CategoricalIndex, Series(..., dtype="category")
  Properties (5):  .categories, .codes, .ordered, .dtype, .name
  Mutation (7):    .add_categories, .remove_categories, .remove_unused_categories,
                   .rename_categories, .reorder_categories, .set_categories,
                   .as_ordered / .as_unordered
  Comparison (4):  __eq__, __ne__, .equals, ordered-comparison (<, >, <=, >=)
  Ordering (4):    .sort_values, .argsort, .min, .max
  Set ops (3):     union_categoricals, Categorical.difference, .isin
  Conversion (4):  .to_list, .tolist, .astype, .map
  Accessor (15+):  .cat.categories, .cat.codes, .cat.add_categories,
                   .cat.rename_categories, .cat.remove_unused_categories, etc.
  GroupBy interaction: groupby(cat_col, observed=True/False), unobserved-category handling
  Concat interaction: pd.concat([cat1, cat2]) — union_categoricals semantics
  Arithmetic interaction: "By design (matches pandas)" per README — but DISC-003 says "tests check error category" — do we test that arithmetic on Categorical raises TypeError like pandas does? Uncovered.

**Gap.** ~50+ categorical-relevant pandas methods/properties; **we have fixtures for 1**. Coverage ratio at this feature area: ~2%.

**Cross-reference with existing code:**
- fp-frame/src/lib.rs:45 defines pub struct CategoricalMetadata
- fp-frame/src/lib.rs:6369 has `Series::cat()` returning Option<CategoricalAccessor>
- fp-frame/src/lib.rs:8993 defines pub struct CategoricalAccessor
- fp-frame/src/lib.rs:8998+ has impl CategoricalAccessor with the accessor methods

The Rust SIDE has ~15 public cat accessor methods. The CONFORMANCE SIDE has 1 fixture.

**Skill rule violated:** "Before claiming conformance, prove it" + Coverage Accounting Matrix (unknown gaps = not conformant per the skill). We can't answer "does our Categorical match pandas behavior on groupby(..., observed=False)?" because we've never tested it.

**Severity.** HIGH — Categorical is called out in the README hero row. Currently the conformance test equivalent is "we export an API named .cat(), it returns Some." A user porting code that uses cat-specific semantics (ordered=True sort, set_categories re-indexing) hits divergence.

**Fix shape:**

1. Generate a `FP-P2D-CAT-NNN` packet suite with fixtures per categorical operation class. First-wave priorities (matching pandas docs' method signatures):
   - FP-P2D-CAT-001..010: constructor variants (Categorical with ordered=True/False, explicit categories, from_codes, dtype string "category")
   - FP-P2D-CAT-011..020: mutation methods (add_categories on ordered vs unordered, remove_unused_categories semantics for NaN codes)
   - FP-P2D-CAT-021..030: comparison (equals across ordering, equals across category-order differences)
   - FP-P2D-CAT-031..040: sort_values (ordered=True respects category order, ordered=False falls back to lex order)
   - FP-P2D-CAT-041..050: groupby interaction (observed=True drops unused; observed=False keeps empty groups — pandas 2.1+ changed this default)
   - FP-P2D-CAT-051..060: concat / union (dtype-consistent categories, mismatched categories raise or coerce)
   - FP-P2D-CAT-061..070: error-path (arithmetic on Categorical → TypeError; tie to br-niwb error coverage)

2. Oracle extension: pandas_oracle.py currently has no op_series_categorical_* handlers beyond from_codes. Add the ~30 new op_* handlers in a single pass tied to br-urhy (oracle tests) — the same commit can add unit-test coverage for each handler.

3. Live-oracle conformance tests in fp-conformance/src/lib.rs: one `live_oracle_series_cat_*_matches_pandas` test per op family.

4. Update README capability row once the new fixtures land:
   `Categorical dtype | Yes | Yes | Yes (metadata + conformance parity; 50+ fixtures across ordered, unordered, groupby-observed, union semantics)`.

5. DISCREPANCIES.md entry if any specific pandas-Categorical corner is intentionally NOT matched (e.g. pandas 2.1's `observed=True` default change — we may intentionally stay on `observed=False` for stability).

Blast radius: ~60-70 new tiny fixtures (~150KB total), ~30 new Python oracle handlers (~400 lines in pandas_oracle.py), ~30 new Rust live-oracle tests (~1200 lines in fp-conformance/src/lib.rs). No changes to fp-frame source.

Prerequisites:
- br-d6xa (CI pandas install) for live-oracle tests to actually run in CI.
- br-urhy (oracle tests) — the ~30 new handlers need their own test coverage per handler.

**Related:**
- br-zk1j (API matrix) — Categorical is a large "Partial" entry today; becomes "Full" when this bead lands.
- br-nato (RequirementLevel) — most Categorical ops are MUST (users catch specific behavior); tag accordingly.
- br-niwb (error-path coverage) — the arithmetic-on-Categorical error path belongs to the 7.7%→30% uplift niwb proposes.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-22T20:59:54.207087695Z","created_by":"cc-pandas","updated_at":"2026-04-22T20:59:54.207087695Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["categorical","conformance","coverage","review"]}

 {"id":"frankenpandas-auwr","title":"fp-conformance: live pandas oracle for DataFrameGroupBy.resample first|last","description":"Add mock-free live-oracle tests for grouped monthly resample first() and last(), asserting stable group/bucket order and missing-value skipping semantics. Exercise the real pandas adapter and persist phase2c evidence for parity drift.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","estimated_minutes":60,"created_at":"2026-04-22T05:29:48.223985670Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-22T06:26:48.785195299Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T06:26:48.783249575Z","close_reason":"shipped 5d11ed6: 3 fp-frame tests (groupby_resample_first_last_int64_input_preserves_value_dtype, groupby_resample_first_last_all_null_bucket_emits_nan, groupby_resample_first_eq_last_for_single_row_buckets) complementary to a7v2's float-input baseline. Locks Int64 dtype preservation, all-NaN bucket NaN-emission, and single-row bucket first==last invariant. Clippy clean.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["conformance","testing"]}
+{"id":"frankenpandas-auys","title":"[HIGH] fuzz: no memory/time bounds on fuzz harness — crafted input can OOM-kill or timeout-kill silently","description":"Applying /testing-fuzzing skill's "No input size guards → OOM kills mask real bugs" anti-pattern + the related OUTPUT-size gap.

**Observation.** Every existing fuzz target has an INPUT size cap:
  fuzz_csv_parse.rs:        MAX_CSV_BYTES    = 64 KB
  fuzz_fixture_parse.rs:    MAX_FIXTURE_BYTES = 64 KB
  fuzz_feather_io.rs:       MAX_FEATHER_BYTES = 512 KB
  fuzz_excel_io.rs:         MAX_EXCEL_BYTES   = 512 KB
  fuzz_dataframe_eval.rs:   MAX_EVAL_FUZZ_BYTES = 256 KB
  etc.

Good — that's the skill's minimum defense (anti-pattern #2 fixed). But there is NO corresponding cap on:
- Peak allocation during parse (the parser can decompress / expand / materialize memory much larger than the input).
- Result DataFrame size (a parser that produces a 1B-row frame from a 64KB input is a logic bug but not a panic).
- Total wall-clock time per invocation (an O(n²) parser on a 64KB input at 10^9 ops = minutes).

**Concrete amplification vectors to worry about:**
- `zip`-compressed Excel/.xlsx payloads — a 512KB xlsx can legitimately decompress to 500 MB of sharedStrings XML. calamine's Excel reader mallocs during decompress.
- Deeply nested JSON in read_json_str — 256 KB of {"a":{"a":{"a":... reaches thousands of levels; serde_json can stack-overflow or allocate per-level frame. Our MAX_EVAL_FUZZ_BYTES cap helps with the INPUT dimension but not with the recursion-depth / heap-alloc dimension.
- Parquet rowgroup metadata that claims 2B rows in a single group — parquet reader attempts to preallocate a Vec<Int64> of that size → OOM.
- SQL queries (if fuzzed per sibling br-gpxk) constructing huge result sets via cross-join.

**Skill rules involved:**
- Anti-pattern #2: "No input size guards → OOM kills mask real bugs." Partially addressed (input guard exists) but the skill's intent is that we should also have a memory-limit / alloc-hook so OOM doesn't kill the fuzzer silently.
- Rule 1: 1000 exec/s minimum for parsers, 100 for stateful. Long-running crash inputs (not-quite-hang, not-quite-crash) violate this by slowing exec/s without emitting a crash. Time-limit the harness.

**Severity.** HIGH — we don't detect memory-DoS or time-DoS via crafted input. Real users running our library against untrusted data (a common pattern: an analytics pipeline accepting customer-uploaded CSVs) could be hit with a denial-of-service that our fuzz corpus can't surface. The README's fp-io positioning as a drop-in pandas reader means users will point it at adversarial inputs.

**Fix shape:**

1. New global wrapper macro / helper applied at the top of every fuzz_target! body:
   ```rust
   const MAX_ALLOC_PER_RUN: usize = 16 * 1024 * 1024;   // 16 MB per invocation
   const MAX_WALL_CLOCK: Duration = Duration::from_millis(500);

   fuzz_target!(|data: &[u8]| {
       let _guard = cap_memory_and_time(MAX_ALLOC_PER_RUN, MAX_WALL_CLOCK);
       if data.len() > MAX_EXCEL_BYTES { return; }
       let _ = fp_conformance::fuzz_excel_io_bytes(data);
   });
   ```

2. `cap_memory_and_time` implementation options:
   - libFuzzer-level: `-rss_limit_mb=16 -timeout=1` flags. Simple, no code change beyond fuzz/Cargo.toml per-bin args.
   - Custom global allocator wrapping `std::alloc::System` with a runtime-accumulated byte counter; returns AllocError when over budget. Integrates with the invariant-checker pattern. More intrusive but catches logic bugs, not just DoS.
   - Both. libFuzzer flags for coarse RSS cap + tracking allocator for fine-grained tracking per test case.

3. For CSV/Excel/Parquet targets: add a per-format cap on the reader's internal `with_capacity` calls. Upstream crates (calamine, parquet) already accept bounds via config — route the bound through.

4. Regression test: once the cap is in place, add a "known-bomb" test case (handcrafted file that attempts to OOM the old path). Include as a permanent fuzz seed so the cap is always exercised.

Blast radius: fuzz/Cargo.toml (+~8 lines: rss_limit_mb + timeout per [[bin]]), new helper in fuzz/src/cap.rs (+~30 lines), 5-10 crafted regression-bomb seeds added to corpus. No changes to fp-* source.

**Related:**
- br-zjme (fuzz CI) — the CI job needs to surface OOM / timeout as distinct failure categories (not silent kills).
- br-liai (differential vs pandas) — the differential oracle's python subprocess has its own memory policy; need to align to avoid Rust-succeeds / python-OOMs divergence.
- br-k05t (scale fixtures) — conformance at scale; sibling goal but separate mechanism (conformance tests, not fuzz).","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-22T20:57:19.730780370Z","created_by":"cc-pandas","updated_at":"2026-04-22T20:57:19.730780370Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["dos","fuzz","memory-bounds","review"]}

Current fp-frame to_markdown rejects this tabulate-compatible format. Add fancy_grid rendering, preserve existing github/pipe/grid/simple/psql/rst behavior, and add exact-output regression tests.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-21T18:01:49.475210008Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-21T18:29:42.352546780Z","closed_at":"2026-04-21T18:29:42.350716685Z","close_reason":"Completed","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

Current fp-frame pivot_table supports median for base cells but pivot_table_with_margins and pivot_table_with_margins_name hard-reject it. Extend margin row/column aggregation to median semantics and add focused regression coverage for All row / All column outputs.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-21T17:59:59.781249413Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-21T18:37:09.750273185Z","closed_at":"2026-04-21T18:37:09.748401161Z","close_reason":"Completed","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
+{"id":"frankenpandas-i9rj","title":"[HIGH] fuzz: no corpus minimization cadence (cargo fuzz cmin / tmin) — Rule 8 violation; corpora will bloat once zjme ships","description":"Applying /testing-fuzzing skill Rule 8: "A corpus with 100K entries and the same coverage as 500 entries is 200x slower, not 200x better. Corpus minimization is not optional — it is a correctness requirement for efficient fuzzing."

**Observation.** grep for `cargo fuzz cmin`, `cargo fuzz tmin`, `afl-cmin`, or any "corpus.*minim" text across scripts/, .github/, AGENTS.md, and fuzz/ returns ZERO hits. No documented cadence. No scheduled CI job. No script that wraps the minimization.

Separately confirmed: `fuzz/corpus/` doesn't exist (per br-zjme findings earlier in this review session) so today the point is moot — there's nothing to minimize yet. But once br-zjme ships persistent corpora, the minimization discipline needs to be in place, otherwise corpora grow unboundedly and fuzz exec/s collapses.

**Skill rules involved:**
- Rule 8: minimize-corpus is a correctness requirement.
- Rule 9: "Minimize BEFORE debugging. Always. cargo fuzz tmin / afl-tmin first." — applies to crash artifacts. A 4KB crash input is unreadable; a 23-byte input often makes the bug obvious.
- Checklist: "Minimize weekly (CRITICAL — bloated corpus wastes cycles)."

**Consequence once corpora exist.** Weekly fuzz runs accumulate corpora:
- libFuzzer's `-merge=1` deduplicates by coverage bitmap at each run (good) but doesn't minimize INPUT SIZE.
- Over weeks the corpus grows from 500 → 50K entries. Individual exec/s remains stable but TOTAL coverage-per-wall-clock degrades because the fuzzer spends time replaying high-coverage paths.
- Crash triage (Rule 9): without `cargo fuzz tmin`, each crash artifact is the byte-for-byte input that tripped libFuzzer. These are typically 100-4000 bytes. Minimizing to the smallest reproducer (5-50 bytes) is what makes the bug obvious on inspection.

**Severity.** HIGH — once the fuzz CI (br-zjme) lands, this bead is the force multiplier that keeps it efficient. Without minimization, zjme ships a slowly-degrading system.

**Fix shape:**

1. `scripts/fuzz_minimize.sh` — runs per-target:
   ```bash
   for target in $(ls fuzz/fuzz_targets/); do
     target=${target%.rs}
     # Minimize each crash artifact down
     for crash in fuzz/artifacts/$target/crash-*; do
       cargo +nightly fuzz tmin "$target" "$crash"
     done
     # Minimize corpus to shrink file count
     cargo +nightly fuzz cmin "$target"
   done
   ```
   Runs weekly via a scheduled CI workflow. Commits the result back via an automated PR.

2. Crash triage pipeline:
   - New pre-PR hook: when a PR adds a fuzz artifact under fuzz/artifacts/, CI runs `cargo fuzz tmin` on it automatically and commits the minimized form.
   - PR reviewers see the minimized artifact; the original is preserved under `fuzz/artifacts/originals/` for forensics.

3. AGENTS.md / CONTRIBUTING.md section documenting the flow:
   - "Found a crash? cargo fuzz tmin first. Then cargo fuzz run one more time against the minimized input to confirm reproducibility. Then commit the minimized input as a regression seed under fuzz/corpus/<target>/regression_<short_hash>."
   - See br-pwn9 sibling (crash-to-regression-test automation).

4. CI budget: tmin + cmin run under rch/remote runners, not the PR gate. Weekly cadence is 20-40 min; PR gate adds only the tmin on new artifacts (~30 s per crash).

Blast radius: 1 new script (~30 lines), 1 new scheduled workflow (~40 lines), 1 new pre-PR hook step, 1 CONTRIBUTING.md paragraph. Zero source changes.

**Related:**
- br-zjme (fuzz CI + persistent corpus) — prerequisite. This bead lands on top once corpora exist.
- br-auys (memory/time caps) — tmin on a hang-until-killed input needs a timeout guard to be useful.
- br-gztr (sanitizer config) — tmin + cmin work on sanitizer campaigns too; all campaigns share the corpus per skill Rule 6.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-22T20:58:04.973964133Z","created_by":"cc-pandas","updated_at":"2026-04-22T20:58:04.973964133Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["corpus","fuzz","minimization","review"]}

Add the double-grid renderer in crates/fp-frame/src/lib.rs and exact-output coverage for index=False and index=True so pandas/tabulate-compatible behavior is frozen.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-21T18:51:44.557953361Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-21T19:10:31.735076476Z","closed_at":"2026-04-21T19:10:31.731565280Z","close_reason":"Completed","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

+{"id":"frankenpandas-lvl6","title":"[HIGH] fuzz: no crash-to-regression-test infrastructure — Rule 10 violation; any found crash will re-regress","description":"Applying /testing-fuzzing skill Rule 10: "Every crash artifact becomes a regression test or it WILL regress. Untested fixes are temporary fixes."

**Observation.** grep for `fn regression_crash_` / `include_bytes!.*crash-` / `include_bytes!.*artifacts` across the entire workspace returns ZERO hits. No `#[test]` fn in any crate replays a fuzz crash artifact against the fixed parser.

Cross-check: the fuzz_*_io_bytes unit tests in fp-conformance/src/lib.rs are POSITIVE seeds (e.g. `fuzz_csv_parse_bytes_accepts_simple_seed_fixture`) — they test that the fuzz harness correctly processes a valid input. They do NOT replay a historical crash.

**Skill rules involved:**
- Rule 10 (direct): every crash becomes a regression test.
- Triage Pipeline step 8: "REGRESSION → Convert to permanent unit test" with an explicit template:
      #[test]
      fn regression_crash_abc123() {
          let input = include_bytes!("../fuzz/artifacts/my_target/crash-abc123");
          let _ = my_crate::parse(input);  // Must not panic after fix
      }

**What this means today.** Zero crashes have ever been locked in as regression tests. Two possibilities:
- (a) Zero crashes have ever been found. Plausible for a young fuzz corpus with no CI gate (br-zjme).
- (b) Crashes have been found and patched but not regressed. Higher-risk assumption.

Either way, the INFRASTRUCTURE for crash → regression is missing:
- No `fuzz/regressions/` directory to store minimized artifacts as per-target permanent seeds.
- No `tests/fuzz_regressions.rs` module with the template-pattern replays.
- No documentation anywhere on the crash triage flow.

**Consequence.** When br-zjme ships fuzz CI, the first real crash will be fixed by a `fix: parser should reject malformed X` commit. Without this bead's infrastructure, the fix is a one-shot patch. Months later, a refactor reintroduces the bug; no test fails. The fuzzer rediscovers it — or doesn't, if the mutation path that originally found it has drifted out of coverage reach.

**Severity.** HIGH — Rule 10 is one of the skill's "Hard Rules (Non-Negotiable)." We don't have the pattern in place, so every crash we ever find can regress. This is force-multiplier for every other fuzz bead in the backlog.

**Fix shape:**

1. `fuzz/regressions/` directory + `fuzz/regressions/README.md`:
   ```
   Minimized crash artifacts that must always reproduce the pre-fix crash
   (and always pass after the fix). One subdirectory per fuzz target.
   ```

2. `crates/fp-conformance/tests/fuzz_regressions.rs` — new integration test file. Pattern per artifact:
   ```rust
   #[test]
   fn regression_csv_parse_unterminated_quote_2026_05_01() {
       let input = include_bytes!(
           "../../../fuzz/regressions/fuzz_csv_parse/unterminated_quote_2026_05_01.csv"
       );
       // Must not panic; may return Err; semantic must match the fix-commit's intent.
       let _ = fp_io::read_csv_str(std::str::from_utf8(input).unwrap_or_default());
   }
   ```

3. Naming convention:
   - Filename: `{target}/{short_description}_{YYYY_MM_DD}.{ext}`
   - Test fn: `regression_{target}_{short_description}_{YYYY_MM_DD}`
   - Commit message: `fix(fp-io): reject malformed X (br-XXXX)\n\nAdds fuzz regression fuzz_csv_parse/unterminated_quote_2026_05_01.csv.`

4. Pre-commit guard (extends existing 50-agent-mail.py hook):
   - If a commit adds a file under `fuzz/regressions/` but not a matching `#[test]` in `fuzz_regressions.rs`, reject.
   - If a commit adds a `fix(fuzz): ...` commit message but no file under `fuzz/regressions/`, warn.

5. Triage workflow doc in AGENTS.md (the section br-6d5s's CONTRIBUTING.md mirrors):
   - Find crash → `cargo fuzz tmin` → save under regressions/ → add regression test → fix bug → verify test passes.

6. CI gate: `cargo test -p fp-conformance --test fuzz_regressions` runs on every PR. Fast (include_bytes! + one parse call per regression = <1s for hundreds of regressions).

Blast radius:
- 1 new directory (empty initially, grows over time).
- 1 new Rust test file (~20 lines + template).
- 1 new README.
- 1 pre-commit hook check (~20 lines of Python extending the existing hook).
- AGENTS.md section (~30 lines).
- CI change: `cargo test` already includes the new test file via `--all-targets`, so no CI.yml change needed.

**Related:**
- br-zjme (fuzz CI + corpus) — prerequisite for crashes to exist.
- br-i9rj (cmin/tmin) — the minimization step that produces the regression artifact.
- br-auys (memory/time caps) — regression tests for "this input used to OOM" need the same caps to avoid re-OOMing in CI.
- br-7cfm (panic contract) — regression tests are concrete examples that the panic contract says what the code does.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-22T20:58:58.967787503Z","created_by":"cc-pandas","updated_at":"2026-04-22T20:58:58.967787503Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["fuzz","regression","review","workflow"]}

 {"id":"frankenpandas-lxhr","title":"[HIGH] maintenance: fp-frame 63k LOC monolith / fp-conformance 32k LOC monolith block parallel-agent workflow","description":"Maintenance-hazard audit on single-source-file sizes.

**Numbers (fresh wc -l):**
- crates/fp-frame/src/lib.rs:         63,391 lines / 2.1 MB
- crates/fp-conformance/src/lib.rs:   32,169 lines / 1.2 MB
- crates/fp-io/src/lib.rs:              7,507 lines / 265 KB

**Observation.** `crates/fp-frame/src/lib.rs` is 63k+ lines in a single flat .rs file — no `mod` decomposition (no `src/series.rs`, `src/dataframe.rs`, `src/groupby.rs`, `src/rolling.rs`, etc.). The module hosts: Series, DataFrame, Column, Rolling, Expanding, Ewm, Resample, DataFrameRolling, DataFrameResample, DataFrameExpanding, SeriesGroupBy, DataFrameGroupBy, GroupByRolling, GroupByResample, plus all tests and proptests inlined.

**Consequences on the active multi-agent workflow:**
1. Every slice I committed today (1zzp.1 through 1zzp.5) edits fp-frame/src/lib.rs. Every parallel-agent commit the same session edits fp-frame/src/lib.rs. Rebase conflicts are constant — I hit two this session (row_multiindex field + group_keys_as_row_multiindex helper) where I had to re-read the file between edits.
2. Incremental `cargo check -p fp-frame` invalidates the whole 2.1 MB file on any edit. On rch, rebuild time is 20-60 s per iteration purely because of file-scope invalidation, not because the change is semantically large.
3. IDE "jump to definition" / "find references" has to parse the 2.1 MB file for every query. rust-analyzer spends ~2-3x longer than comparable-sized workspaces.
4. PR diff review against this file is unusable — GitHub's diff UI drops into "load diff" prompts; 15k-line PRs flatten into noise.
5. `git blame` for any line requires loading the entire file history; forks of history intersect heavily.

**Same problem, smaller scale, in fp-conformance/src/lib.rs (32k lines)** — holds the fixture runner, 89 live_oracle_* tests, the bootstrap_cases harness, plus all fuzz_*_bytes harnesses (4 of them inlined: 5431-5579 alone is fuzz_dataframe_eval + fuzz_semantic_eq).

**Severity.** HIGH — this is the single largest drag on parallel-agent velocity this session. The issue is structural, not cosmetic: every future slice of any epic (row-MultiIndex was just one of several planned) will compound the conflict surface.

**Fix shape:**

fp-frame split (mandatory order; each step compiles green):
1. `src/column.rs` — Column + ValidityMask + Column::from_values (lives at lib.rs:~3000-4000).
2. `src/series/`        mod with mod.rs + arith.rs + rolling.rs + expanding.rs + ewm.rs (Series-impl blocks currently at lib.rs:~993-6000).
3. `src/dataframe/`     mod with mod.rs + constructors.rs + io_ext.rs + arith.rs + apply.rs + indexing.rs + reshape.rs (DataFrame impl blocks at ~14120-16000).
4. `src/groupby/`       mod with mod.rs + series_groupby.rs + dataframe_groupby.rs + rolling.rs + resample.rs (at ~25782-28074 plus SeriesGroupBy blocks earlier).
5. `src/rolling.rs` + `src/resample.rs` (shared Rolling/Resample builders).
6. Tests move to `src/tests/` mod or co-located `#[cfg(test)]` mod inside each submodule.

fp-conformance split (parallel-safe):
1. `src/harness/` mod: fixture loader, comparator (compare_series_expected / compare_dataframe_expected), oracle dispatch.
2. `src/fuzz/` mod: all fuzz_*_bytes harnesses (4+ of them, which is where br-frankenpandas-liai [diff fuzz target] will land naturally).
3. `src/tests/live_oracle.rs` — the 89 live_oracle_*_matches_pandas tests.
4. `src/tests/packet_suite.rs` — run_packet_suite + run_e2e_suite.

Blast radius: large but mechanical. Split produces structural improvement, no behavior change. Each sub-bead is a separate PR (file by file) so reviews stay tractable.

Prerequisites: none. But should land BEFORE any further epic decomposition (e.g. the fd90 SQL backend epic's 7 slices would otherwise pile onto fp-io as the next monolith).","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-22T19:58:51.801544673Z","created_by":"cc-pandas","updated_at":"2026-04-22T19:58:51.801544673Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["maintenance","modularity","review","workflow"]}
